approach_notes.text: Per map evidence: OB describes specific approaches (e.g. lateral for bimalleolar) but catalog gaps prevent full recommended IDs. No free-form guessing; limited to available (e.g. posteromedial for some ankle variants).

review_reason: OB (trauma/1047) specifies direct lateral approach to fibula + direct medial approach to ankle for bimalleolar; also lists dedicated posterolateral and posteromedial for variants with posterior involvement. Dedicated 'Approach to the Lateral Malleolus' page exists. Catalog has no lateral fibula or dedicated medial malleolus ID (only anterior + posteromedial ankle). Anterior ankle explicitly for joint/talus access, not routine bimalleolar. Map therefore leaves recommended empty + blocks anterior; manual_review required until catalog expanded.

Catalog only has generic anterior + posteromedial; no pilon-specific lateral or combined. Blocked anterior as default for many patterns. manual_review.

review_reason: OB calcaneus topic describes extensile lateral approach (L-shaped) as classic for ORIF, plus sinus tarsi and other limited. No matching catalog ID for extensile lateral calcaneus or foot-specific. manual_review.

conditional_approach_ids.condition: dorsal fragment or dorsal plate or dorsal comminution or dorsal approach indicated

important_anatomy.text: Distal radius responsible for 80% of axial load through wrist. Articulates with scaphoid via scaphoid fossa, lunate via lunate fossa, distal ulna via ulnar/sigmoid notch. Comprised of three columns: radial (radial styloid/scaphoid fossa; attachments for brachioradialis, long radiolunate, radioscaphocapitate ligaments), intermediate (lunate fossa), ulnar (TFCC and distal ulna for DRUJ stability and forearm rotation).

Lister's tubercle on dorsal surface ~1 cm proximal to articular surface; EPL tendon lies just ulnar to it before sharp turn into thumb. Important landmark to avoid EPL attritional rupture with long volar plate screws.

Watershed line: most volar and prominent aspect of distal radius (lunate portion most prominent with short radiolunate ligament attachment). Critical region to remain proximal to for volar plate fixation to avoid FPL rupture.
Internervous plane in FCR approach between FCR (median n.) and FPL (AIN).

structures_at_risk.structure: Palmar cutaneous branch of median nerve
structures_at_risk.why_it_matters: Arises 5 cm proximal to wrist joint; runs ulnar to FCR. Injury causes thenar numbness or neuroma.
Cannot ligate if poor ulnar contribution on Allen's test. At risk during retraction.
Do not remove from volar distal radius unless joint access needed; errant release leads to radiocarpal instability.
At risk in Henry-type or dorsal approaches; protect with forearm position and subperiosteal dissection.
Vulnerable in superficial dissection; injury causes painful neuroma or sensory loss.

landmarks.why_it_matters: Primary landmark for volar incision and interval development.
Dorsal landmark; guides EPL protection and dorsal approaches.
Attachment for key ligaments; reference for radial column fixation.
Most volar prominence; stay proximal with volar plates to protect FPL.

approach_notes.text: Volar FCR approach: incision along FCR tendon sheath (ulnar/radial curve at crease), develop plane, retract FCR ulnarly, incise FCR sheath dorsally, retract FPL ulnarly, incise PQ borders and elevate. Proximal/distal extensions as needed for median n. or scaphoid.
Dorsal approach useful for displaced intra-articular dorsal lip fx or carpal work; no true internervous plane, between 3rd/4th extensor compartments.
Volar plating preferred; plate must not be placed distal to watershed line to avoid FPL irritation/rupture. Assess screw penetration with skyline and 23° elevated lateral views.

review_reason: OB pelvis fractures describe anterior (Pfannenstiel/Stoppa/ilioinguinal variants) and posterior (KL or percutaneous) depending on instability pattern. Catalog has ilioinguinal and KL but no dedicated anterior pelvic ring or Stoppa ID. Limited mapping possible; many percutaneous. manual_review for open cases.

important_anatomy.text: Key intervals: posterior shares with Kocher-Langenbeck (gluteus maximus split, short ERs). Anterior: TFL/sartorius then rectus/gluteus medius. Lateral: no true internervous, splits abductors.

structures_at_risk.why_it_matters: Runs between gluteus medius and minimus 3-5 cm above GT; protect by limiting proximal split of medius and using stay suture at apex of split. Injury causes Trendelenburg gait.

approach_notes.text: Direct lateral (Hardinge): split IT band, split gluteus medius/vastus lateralis (limit proximal split to 3-5cm above GT to protect superior gluteal n.). Detach anterior gluteus medius/minimus and vastus lateralis anteriorly.

pimp_topics: What nerve is at risk in direct lateral (Hardinge) approach and how far proximal can you safely split the gluteus medius?
Describe the short external rotators and their role in sciatic nerve protection during posterior approach.
Why repair short external rotators after posterior THA?

important_anatomy.text: Key intervals: posterior shares with Kocher-Langenbeck (gluteus maximus split, short ERs). Anterior: TFL/sartorius then rectus/gluteus medius. Lateral: no true internervous, splits abductors.

structures_at_risk.why_it_matters: Initially along posterior quadratus femoris; protect by ER release and posterior retraction of short rotators. Injury more common to peroneal division; may need AFO.
Leaves pelvis below piriformis; if cut and retracts, may require internal iliac ligation.

landmarks.landmark: Greater trochanter and short external rotators (piriformis, obturator internus)
landmarks.why_it_matters: Key for interval and sciatic protection; tag and release close to insertion.

approach_notes.text: Posterior (Moore/Southern): lateral position, incision posterior to GT, split gluteus maximus, detach short ERs (piriformis, obturator internus) with stay sutures and reflect to protect sciatic n., T-capsulotomy, dislocate with IR. Repair short ERs to reduce dislocation risk.

approach_notes.text: Direct anterior (Smith-Petersen): interval between TFL and sartorius superficially, rectus femoris and gluteus medius deeply. Advantages: abductor sparing, lower dislocation rate in studies.

important_anatomy.text: Medial parapatellar provides excellent exposure to entire knee joint. Infrapatellar branch of saphenous nerve at risk (sensory to anteromedial knee).

structures_at_risk.why_it_matters: Crosses field; injury causes painful neuroma or numbness anteromedial knee. Often sacrificed or protected in medial parapatellar.

approach_notes.text: Medial parapatellar arthrotomy is the most common/standard primary TKA approach. Midline incision, develop medial skin flap, medial parapatellar arthrotomy, evert patella laterally. Variations (midvastus, subvastus) preserve more quad but less extensile.

important_anatomy.text: Femur is largest/strongest bone with anterior bow. Linea aspera on posterior middle third is attachment for muscles/fascia and acts as compressive strut. Thigh has three compartments: anterior (quads, sartorius), posterior (hamstrings), adductor (adductors, gracilis).
Deforming forces: proximal fragment abducted (gluteus med/min) and flexed (iliopsoas); distal segment varus (adductors) and extension (gastrocnemius on posterior distal femur).

structures_at_risk.why_it_matters: At risk in open approaches or with medial butterfly fragments; significant blood loss (1-1.5L closed, more open).

approach_notes.text: Lateral approach standard for ORIF/plating: patient lateral or supine, incision along lateral thigh, split IT band, develop plane between vastus lateralis and biceps (posterolateral) or elevate VL. Subperiosteal elevation.
